GCD identities for exponential sequences - Buffed Seed

Let $p=19$, $k=4$, $M=p^k=19^4$, and $a=20$.

For each integer $t\ge 1$, define
$G_t=\gcd\!\left(20^{65t+6234}-1,\ 20^{16t+1429}-1\right)$.

1. Prove that the set $\{t\ge 1:\ 19^4\mid G_t\}$ is nonempty and that for all $t\ge 1$,
$19^4\mid G_t \iff \mathrm{ord}_{19^4}(20)\mid \gcd(65t+6234,\ 16t+1429) \iff 19^3\mid(65t+6234)$.

2. Let $t_0$ be the least positive integer $t$ such that $19^4\mid G_t$.
Report the unique integer $N$ with $0\le N<1000$ and $N\equiv t_0\pmod{1000}$.

Solution.
Step 1 (Euclidean_algorithm_on_exponents): For integers $X,Y$ and any integer $q$, we have $\gcd(X,Y)=\gcd(X,Y-qX)$ because an integer divides both $X$ and $Y$ iff it divides both $X$ and $Y-qX$. In particular, taking $q=\lfloor Y/X\rfloor$ gives the remainder form $\gcd(X,Y)=\gcd(X,\,Y\bmod X)$.
Step 2 (Euclidean_algorithm_on_exponents): Let $A>1$ and $u,v\in\mathbb N$. Write $v=qu+r$ with $0\le r<u$. Then
$A^v-1=A^{qu+r}-1=(A^u)^qA^r-1=(A^u-1)\cdot\big((A^u)^{q-1}A^r+\cdots+A^r\big)+(A^r-1)$,
so $\gcd(A^u-1,A^v-1)=\gcd(A^u-1,A^r-1)$ by Step 1. Iterating Euclid remainders on $(u,v)$ until the exponent becomes $\gcd(u,v)$ yields
$\gcd(A^u-1,A^v-1)=A^{\gcd(u,v)}-1$ (from Step 1).
Step 3 (order_arguments_mod_prime_power): For $\gcd(A,M)=1$, define $\mathrm{ord}_M(A)$ as the least positive $d$ with $A^d\equiv 1\pmod M$. Then $M\mid (A^t-1)$ iff $\mathrm{ord}_M(A)\mid t$: if $t=qd+r$ with $0\le r<d$, then $A^t\equiv (A^d)^qA^r\equiv A^r\pmod M$, so $M\mid(A^t-1)$ forces $M\mid(A^r-1)$ and hence $r=0$ by minimality of $d$; conversely $d\mid t$ gives $A^t\equiv 1\pmod M$.
Step 4 (order_arguments_mod_prime_power): We compute $\mathrm{ord}_{19^4}(20)$. Since $20\equiv 1+19\pmod{19^4}$, it suffices to show $\mathrm{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}$ for odd prime $p$ and $k\ge 2$, then specialize to $(p,k)=(19,4)$.

Let $k\ge 2$ and set $n=p^{k-1}$. By the binomial theorem,
$(1+p)^n-1=\binom{n}{1}p+\sum_{j\ge 2}\binom{n}{j}p^j$.
The first term is $\binom{n}{1}p=np=p^k$.

For $j\ge 2$, write $\binom{n}{j}=\frac{n}{j}\binom{n-1}{j-1}$. Hence
$v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{j}\right)=v_p(n)-v_p(j)+v_p\!\left(\binom{n-1}{j-1}\right)\ge (k-1)-v_p(j)$,
so
$v_p\!\left(\binom{n}{j}p^j\right)\ge (k-1)-v_p(j)+j=(k-1)+(j-v_p(j))\ge k$,
because $j\ge 2$ implies $j-v_p(j)\ge 1$. Therefore every $j\ge 2$ term is divisible by $p^k$, so $(1+p)^{p^{k-1}}\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$.

Now set $n'=p^{k-2}$ and expand $(1+p)^{n'}-1=\binom{n'}{1}p+\sum_{j\ge 2}\binom{n'}{j}p^j=p^{k-1}+\sum_{j\ge 2}\binom{n'}{j}p^j$.
For $j\ge 2$, the same bound gives
$v_p\!\left(\binom{n'}{j}p^j\right)\ge (k-2)-v_p(j)+j=(k-2)+(j-v_p(j))\ge k$,
since for odd $p$ and $j\ge 2$ we have $v_p(j)\le j-2$ (because $p^{j-1}>j$) and thus $j-v_p(j)\ge 2$.
Hence all $j\ge 2$ terms are divisible by $p^k$, so $(1+p)^{p^{k-2}}\equiv 1+p^{k-1}\not\equiv 1\pmod{p^k}$.

Thus $p^{k-1}$ is an exponent giving $1$ mod $p^k$, but $p^{k-2}$ is not; since every divisor of $p^{k-1}$ is a power of $p$, this forces $\mathrm{ord}_{p^k}(1+p)=p^{k-1}$. Specializing gives $\mathrm{ord}_{19^4}(20)=19^3=6859$.
Step 5 (Euclidean_algorithm_on_exponents): Put $E_1:=65t+6234$ and $E_2:=16t+1429$, and let $g_t:=\gcd(E_1,E_2)$. Applying the identity from Step 2 with $A=20$, $u=E_1$, $v=E_2$ gives
$G_t=\gcd(20^{E_1}-1,\ 20^{E_2}-1)=20^{g_t}-1$ (from Step 2).
Step 6 (order_to_Bezout_bridge): By Step 5, $19^4\mid G_t$ is equivalent to $20^{g_t}\equiv 1\pmod{19^4}$. Since $\gcd(20,19^4)=1$, Step 3 applies and yields
$19^4\mid G_t \iff \mathrm{ord}_{19^4}(20)\mid g_t=\gcd(E_1,E_2)$ (from Step 3 and from Step 5).
Step 7 (Bezout_on_linear_exponents): Any common divisor of $E_1$ and $E_2$ divides any integer linear combination, so it divides
$65E_2-16E_1=65\cdot 1429-16\cdot 6234=-6859$.
Hence $g_t=\gcd(E_1,E_2)$ is a divisor of $6859$.
Step 8 (Bezout_on_linear_exponents): Let $d:=\gcd(E_1,6859)$. Then $d\mid E_1$ and $d\mid 6859=65E_2-16E_1$, so $d\mid 65E_2$. Since $\gcd(65,6859)=1$, we get $d\mid E_2$, so $d$ is a common divisor of $E_1,E_2$ and thus $d\le g_t$. Conversely, from Step 7 we have $g_t\mid 6859$, hence $g_t\le \gcd(E_1,6859)=d$. Therefore $g_t=d=\gcd(65t+6234,6859)$ (from Step 7).
Step 9 (order_to_Bezout_bridge): Insert $\mathrm{ord}_{19^4}(20)=6859$ from Step 4 into Step 6 to get
$19^4\mid G_t \iff 6859\mid g_t$.
Using $g_t\mid 6859$ and $g_t=\gcd(65t+6234,6859)$ from Step 8, this holds iff $\gcd(65t+6234,6859)=6859$, i.e. iff $6859\mid(65t+6234)$. This proves
$19^4\mid G_t \iff \mathrm{ord}_{19^4}(20)\mid \gcd(E_1,E_2) \iff 19^3\mid(65t+6234)$ (from Step 4, from Step 6, and from Step 8),
and it also shows the set of such $t$ is nonempty.
Step 10 (order_to_Bezout_bridge): The condition from Step 9 is the linear congruence
$65t\equiv -6234\pmod{6859}$.
Because $\gcd(65,6859)=1$, there is exactly one residue class $t\pmod{6859}$ satisfying it: if $65t_1\equiv 65t_2\pmod{6859}$, then $6859\mid 65(t_1-t_2)$ forces $6859\mid (t_1-t_2)$.
Step 11 (Bezout_on_linear_exponents): Compute the inverse of $65$ modulo $6859$ by the extended Euclidean algorithm:
$6859=65\cdot 105+34$, $65=34\cdot 1+31$, $34=31\cdot 1+3$, $31=3\cdot 10+1$.
Back-substitution gives $1=2216\cdot 65-21\cdot 6859$, so $65^{-1}\equiv 2216\pmod{6859}$ (from Step 10).
Step 12 (order_to_Bezout_bridge): Multiply $65t\equiv -6234\pmod{6859}$ by $2216$ from Step 11:
$t\equiv -6234\cdot 2216\pmod{6859}$.
Compute $6234\cdot 2216=13,814,544=6859\cdot 2014+518$, so $t\equiv -518\equiv 6341\pmod{6859}$.
Thus all solutions are $t=6341+6859q$, and the least positive one is $t_0=6341$ (from Step 11).
Step 13 (order_to_Bezout_bridge): The requested output is $N\equiv t_0\pmod{1000}$ with $0\le N<1000$. Using $t_0=6341$ from Step 12, we get $N\equiv 6341\equiv 341\pmod{1000}$, so $N=341$.
Answer: 341

3) Uniqueness Test
From Step 9, $19^4\mid G_t$ holds exactly when $65t+6234\equiv 0\pmod{6859}$.

Since $\gcd(65,6859)=1$, Step 10 implies this congruence has exactly one residue class modulo $6859$, hence exactly one least positive solution $t_0$. Step 12 gives that solution as $t_0=6341$, so the normalized output $t_0\bmod 1000$ is uniquely forced to be $341$.

Collision check (NT6): If one mistakenly replaces the forced order $19^3$ by $19^2$, the wrong-branch condition becomes $65t+6234\equiv 0\pmod{19^2}$ with least solution $t=204$, giving $204\not\equiv 341\pmod{1000}$.

341
